title: Coroutines Deep Dive
description: Scopes, structured concurrency, exception handling, and advanced patterns
lesson:
Coroutine Scopes & Structured Concurrency

Every coroutine runs in a scope that defines its lifetime. A scope holds a Job (tracks children) and a CoroutineContext (dispatcher, error handler). The scope creates a parent-child hierarchy with three rules:

The Three Rules of Structured Concurrency:
1. Parent cancelled → all children cancelled
2. Child fails → parent cancelled (unless SupervisorJob)
3. Parent waits for all children before completing
// ViewModel scope — cancelled when ViewModel is cleared viewModelScope.launch { launch { fetchUser() } // child 1 launch { fetchPosts() } // child 2 // Parent waits for BOTH to finish }

launch vs async

launchasync
ReturnsJob (no result)Deferred<T> (a future result)
Use forSide effects (update UI, write DB)Parallel computation needing results
ExceptionsPropagate UP to parent immediatelyHeld until .await() is called
// Parallel decomposition: both run concurrently val user = async { api.getUser(id) } // starts immediately val posts = async { api.getPosts(id) } // starts immediately showProfile(user.await(), posts.await()) // total = slowest, not sum

Exception Handling — The Tricky Part

launch { throw Exception() } │ ▼ Exception propagates UP to parent │ ▼ Parent cancels ALL children (siblings die too) │ ▼ If no handler → app crashes ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ async { throw Exception() } │ ▼ Exception is HELD inside Deferred │ ▼ Re-thrown when .await() is called │ ▼ Can be caught with try { deferred.await() } catch { }
CoroutineExceptionHandler only works on the root coroutine (first launch in the scope). On child coroutines, the exception propagates to the parent before the child's handler runs. For child error handling, use try/catch.

SupervisorJob — Independent Children

Regular Job: SupervisorJob: ───────────── ────────────── Parent Job Parent (Supervisor) ├── Child A: FAILS ──┐ ├── Child A: FAILS └── Child B: running │ └── Child B: KEEPS RUNNING ✓ ↓ │ CANCELLED by parent ◄┘ Child A's failure does NOT affect siblings

viewModelScope and lifecycleScope use SupervisorJob — you don't want one failed API call to cancel all ViewModel work.

Dispatchers — Thread Pools Underneath

A Dispatcher IS a thread pool. Each one is optimized for different work:

DispatcherThread PoolUse ForJava Equivalent
Dispatchers.MainSingle UI threadTouch views, update StateHandler(Looper.getMainLooper())
Dispatchers.IOUp to 64 threads (elastic)Network, disk, databaseExecutors.newCachedThreadPool()
Dispatchers.Default= CPU cores (fixed)JSON parsing, sorting, encryptionExecutors.newFixedThreadPool(cores)
Dispatchers.UnconfinedResumes on caller's threadRare, testing only—

What Is a Job on the JVM?

Coroutines are NOT JVM magic — they're a compiler + library feature. No special bytecode support.

Kotlin Coroutine JVM / Java Equivalent ──────────────── ──────────────────── CoroutineScope → ExecutorService (thread pool) Job → Future<?> (cancel/check handle) Deferred<T> → CompletableFuture<T> Dispatcher → Executor / ThreadPoolExecutor suspend fun → Callback (compiled to state machine) launch { } → executor.submit(Runnable) async { } → executor.submit(Callable<T>) delay(1000) → ScheduledExecutor.schedule() Channel → BlockingQueue Flow → RxJava Observable

The compiler transforms each suspend fun into a state machine with labels at each suspension point. The Continuation interface is literally just a callback:

// This is the ENTIRE Continuation interface: interface Continuation<in T> { val context: CoroutineContext fun resumeWith(result: Result<T>) } // That's it. A callback with context. No JVM magic.

The + Operator — How Context Combining Works

When you write CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.Main), the + is operator overloading. CoroutineContext works like a Map — each element has a unique key:

SupervisorJob() Dispatchers.Main CoroutineName("X") ┌──────────────┐ ┌───────────────┐ ┌────────────────┐ │ Key: Job │ + │ Key: Intercept│ + │ Key: Name │ │ Val: SvJob │ │ Val: MainDisp │ │ Val: "X" │ └──────────────┘ └───────────────┘ └────────────────┘ │ │ │ └───────────────────────┴─────────────────────────┘ │ ┌────────────────────────┐ │ Combined Context Map: │ │ Job → SupervisorJob │ │ Intercept → Main │ │ Name → "X" │ └────────────────────────┘ Same key? Right side wins: Dispatchers.IO + Dispatchers.Main → Main (same key: Interceptor) Job() + SupervisorJob() → SupervisorJob (same key: Job)

Built-In Scopes Reference

ScopeWhereCancelled WhenDispatcherJob Type
viewModelScopeViewModelonCleared()Main.immediateSupervisorJob
lifecycleScopeActivity/FragmentonDestroy()Main.immediateSupervisorJob
rememberCoroutineScope()ComposableLeaves compositionMain.immediateJob
GlobalScopeAnywhereNeverDefault—
Avoid GlobalScope — it has no lifecycle, so coroutines leak. Always prefer a lifecycle-bound scope.

Creating Custom Scopes for Complex Apps

For an app with multiple subsystems (RAG, voice, token processing), custom scopes are standard practice:

// Each subsystem gets its own scope + appropriate dispatcher class LlmAppManager { private val ragScope = CoroutineScope( SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.IO + CoroutineName("RAG") ) private val voiceScope = CoroutineScope( SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.Main + CoroutineName("Voice") ) private val tokenScope = CoroutineScope( SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.Default + CoroutineName("Tokenizer") ) fun destroy() { ragScope.cancel() // MUST cancel to prevent leaks voiceScope.cancel() tokenScope.cancel() } }

Compared to Java thread pools, coroutine scopes give you: structured parent-child cancellation, automatic cleanup, sequential-looking async code, and thousands of coroutines per thread (they suspend, not block).

Coroutines vs Java Threads — Under the Hood

Coroutines are often called "lightweight threads," but what does that actually mean at the OS level? The differences are fundamental:

Java ThreadKotlin Coroutine
Memory~1 MB stack (fixed at creation)~few hundred bytes (state on heap)
1,000 concurrent≈ 1 GB RAM just for stacks≈ few hundred KB
100,000 concurrent≈ 100 GB — impossible≈ few MB — trivial
SchedulingOS kernel preemptiveUserspace cooperative

What Happens When Suspended?

Thread.sleep() / blocking IO: ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ OS Thread │ │ ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ Stack (1 MB) HELD in memory │ │ │ │ CPU registers saved by kernel │ │ │ │ Thread state BLOCKED │ │ │ └──────────────────────────────────────┘ │ │ Thread cannot run ANYTHING else. │ │ Other coroutines on this thread? STUCK. │ └──────────────────────────────────────────┘ Coroutine suspend (delay, withContext, etc.): ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Coroutine state saved to heap object │ │ (local vars, label = which suspend point)│ │ Size: ~few hundred bytes │ └──────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ OS Thread now FREE │ │ Picks up next coroutine from queue │ │ Runs other work immediately │ └──────────────────────────────────────────┘

Context Switching Cost

Thread context switch (~1–10 µs): Kernel mode transition → save CPU registers → flush TLB/cache → restore next thread’s registers → return to userspace.

Coroutine switch (~100 ns): Swap which state-machine object the thread executes. Entirely in userspace. No kernel involvement, no register save/restore, no cache flush.

withContext() Thread Switching

withContext() only causes an actual thread switch when you change dispatchers:

// NO thread switch — already on IO, staying on IO withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { // same dispatcher = no switch readFile() } } // YES thread switch — moving from IO to Main withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { val data = readFile() withContext(Dispatchers.Main) { // different dispatcher = real thread switch updateUI(data) // cost ≈ same as a thread context switch } }
Key insight: withContext() is cheap when staying on the same dispatcher (just a function call). It’s only expensive when crossing dispatchers, because that requires an actual OS thread switch.

Mutex vs ReentrantLock

Both protect shared mutable state, but they behave very differently in a coroutine world:

ReentrantLock (Java)Mutex (Kotlin Coroutines)
When contendedBLOCKS the threadSUSPENDS the coroutine
Other coroutines on same threadAlso blocked!Thread freed to run them
Reentrant?Yes — same thread can re-acquireNO — deadlocks if re-acquired!
Usable in suspend funs?Risky (blocks thread)Yes (designed for it)
// ReentrantLock — BLOCKS the thread (bad in coroutines) val lock = ReentrantLock() fun updateCounter() { lock.lock() try { counter++ lock.lock() // OK! ReentrantLock allows re-entry by same thread counter++ lock.unlock() } finally { lock.unlock() } } // Mutex — SUSPENDS the coroutine (good in coroutines) val mutex = Mutex() suspend fun updateCounter() { mutex.withLock { counter++ // mutex.withLock { } // DEADLOCK! Mutex is NOT reentrant } }
CRITICAL: Mutex is NOT reentrant. If a coroutine holding a Mutex calls a suspend function that tries to acquire the same Mutex, it will deadlock forever. The coroutine suspends waiting for itself to release the lock — which it never will.
When to use which:
ReentrantLock: Non-suspend code, need reentrant behavior, very short critical sections where suspension overhead > blocking cost.
Mutex: Suspend code, longer critical sections, want to keep threads free for other coroutines.

Thread Pinning & newSingleThreadContext

Dispatchers.Default uses a shared thread pool. A coroutine can move between threads at each suspension point — fine for computation, but problematic for latency-sensitive or thread-affine work.

Dispatchers.Default (thread pool, N = CPU cores): ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ │Thread-1 │ │Thread-2 │ │Thread-3 │ │Thread-4 │ └────┬─────┘ └────┬─────┘ └────┬─────┘ └────┬─────┘ │ │ │ │ Coroutine A runs on Thread-1 │── suspend ──│ │── resume on Thread-2 (moved!) │── suspend ──────────────│ │── resume on Thread-4 (moved again!) newSingleThreadContext("Audio"): ┌──────────────────┐ │ Audio Thread │ ← dedicated OS thread └────────┬─────────┘ │ Coroutine A runs on Audio Thread │── suspend │── resume on Audio Thread (same thread, always) │── suspend │── resume on Audio Thread (pinned)
// Default: coroutine can jump between threads withContext(Dispatchers.Default) { println(Thread.currentThread().name) // "DefaultDispatcher-worker-1" delay(100) println(Thread.currentThread().name) // "DefaultDispatcher-worker-3" (maybe different!) } // Pinned: coroutine stays on one dedicated thread val audioDispatcher = newSingleThreadContext("AudioThread") withContext(audioDispatcher) { println(Thread.currentThread().name) // "AudioThread" processAudioFrame() delay(100) println(Thread.currentThread().name) // "AudioThread" (always same) processNextFrame() } // IMPORTANT: close when done to avoid thread leak audioDispatcher.close()
Use newSingleThreadContext for: Audio processing, hardware interaction, JNI calls that expect the same thread, OpenGL contexts, or any work requiring thread affinity.

Trade-off: Pinning gives predictable cache/register behavior and thread affinity, but dedicates an entire OS thread — don’t create dozens of these.

What the Compiler Actually Generates

A suspend function is transformed by the Kotlin compiler into a state machine. There is no JVM magic, no special bytecode — just a compiler plugin that rewrites your code:

// What you write: suspend fun fetchProfile(id: String): Profile { val user = fetchUser(id) // suspension point 1 val avatar = fetchAvatar(user) // suspension point 2 val posts = fetchPosts(user) // suspension point 3 return Profile(user, avatar, posts) }
// What the compiler generates (simplified): fun fetchProfile(id: String, continuation: Continuation<Profile>): Any? { // State machine object — stores local vars between suspensions class FetchProfileSM : ContinuationImpl(continuation) { var label = 0 // which suspension point are we at? var id: String? = null // saved local variable var user: User? = null // saved local variable var avatar: Avatar? = null } val sm = continuation as? FetchProfileSM ?: FetchProfileSM() when (sm.label) { 0 -> { sm.id = id sm.label = 1 // next time, resume at label 1 val result = fetchUser(id, sm) // pass state machine as callback if (result == COROUTINE_SUSPENDED) return COROUTINE_SUSPENDED } 1 -> { sm.user = sm.result as User // retrieve result from callback sm.label = 2 val result = fetchAvatar(sm.user!!, sm) if (result == COROUTINE_SUSPENDED) return COROUTINE_SUSPENDED } 2 -> { sm.avatar = sm.result as Avatar sm.label = 3 val result = fetchPosts(sm.user!!, sm) if (result == COROUTINE_SUSPENDED) return COROUTINE_SUSPENDED } 3 -> { val posts = sm.result as List<Post> return Profile(sm.user!!, sm.avatar!!, posts) } } }
The Continuation interface IS just a callback: ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ interface Continuation<in T> { │ │ val context: CoroutineContext │ │ fun resumeWith(result: Result<T>) │ │ } │ └─────────────────────────────────────────┘ State saved to heap between suspensions: ┌───────────────────────────┐ │ FetchProfileSM (on heap) │ │ label = 2 │ ← which suspension point │ id = "123" │ ← saved local vars │ user = User(...) │ │ avatar = null │ ← not yet assigned │ result = Avatar(...) │ ← last callback result └───────────────────────────┘ Size: ~few hundred bytes (vs ~1 MB thread stack)
This is why coroutines are “just a library + compiler plugin”:
• No special JVM bytecode instructions
• No runtime support needed beyond the Kotlin stdlib
• The Continuation interface is literally just a callback
• State (local variables) is saved to a small heap object
• The compiler rewrites suspend fun into a state machine with when(label) branches
• This compiles to the same bytecode as any regular Kotlin class

Bridging Coroutines ↔ Callbacks

Real-world Android code often needs to interface with legacy callback-based APIs (Java libraries, platform APIs, Firebase). Here's how to bridge both directions:

Wrapping a One-Shot Callback → suspend

Use suspendCancellableCoroutine to turn any callback into a clean suspend function:

// Legacy Java API interface LegacyApi { fun fetchUser(id: String, callback: UserCallback) } // Wrap into a suspend function suspend fun LegacyApi.fetchUserSuspend(id: String): User { return suspendCancellableCoroutine { continuation -> fetchUser(id, object : UserCallback { override fun onSuccess(user: User) { continuation.resume(user) // Resume with result } override fun onError(error: Exception) { continuation.resumeWithException(error) // Resume with error } }) continuation.invokeOnCancellation { // Clean up if coroutine is cancelled } } } // Now use it like any suspend function: val user = legacyApi.fetchUserSuspend("123") // No callback!

Wrapping a Streaming Callback → Flow

Use callbackFlow for APIs that emit multiple values (sensors, location, Firebase listeners):

// Legacy listener that fires repeatedly fun locationUpdates(manager: LocationManager): Flow<Location> = callbackFlow { val listener = LocationListener { location -> trySend(location) // Emit each callback as a Flow value } manager.registerListener(listener) awaitClose { manager.unregisterListener(listener) } // Cleanup }

Exposing Coroutines → Legacy Callback (for Java callers)

// Java code expects a callback fun getUser(id: String, callback: DataCallback<User>) { scope.launch { try { val user = repo.getUser(id) // suspend function withContext(Dispatchers.Main) { callback.onResult(user) } } catch (e: Exception) { withContext(Dispatchers.Main) { callback.onError(e) } } } } // StateFlow → LiveData (for Java observers): val userLiveData: LiveData<User> = userStateFlow.asLiveData()
Quick Reference:
Callback (one-shot) → suspend: suspendCancellableCoroutine
Callback (streaming) → Flow: callbackFlow
suspend → Callback: scope.launch { callback(result) }
StateFlow → LiveData: .asLiveData()
LiveData → Flow: .asFlow()

questions.title: launch vs async
questions.description: What is the key difference between launch and async?
options: launch runs on Main thread, async runs on IO
launch is for fire-and-forget (returns Job), async returns a Deferred<T> that you .await() for a result
launch is sequential, async is parallel
launch is deprecated in favor of async
questions.explanation: launch starts a coroutine and returns a Job (no result). Use it for side effects — updating UI, writing to DB, sending analytics. async returns Deferred<T> — a promise of a future value. Call .await() to get the result. Use async when you need to run things in parallel and combine results.
hints: Think about return values — one has a result, one does not

questions.title: Parallel Decomposition
questions.description: How does this code execute? What happens and in what order?
questions.code:
viewModelScope.launch {
    val startTime = System.currentTimeMillis()
    
    val user = async { fetchUser() }      // takes 2 seconds
    val posts = async { fetchPosts() }     // takes 3 seconds
    val settings = async { fetchSettings() } // takes 1 second
    
    val profile = Profile(
        user = user.await(),
        posts = posts.await(),
        settings = settings.await()
    )
    
    val elapsed = System.currentTimeMillis() - startTime
    Log.d("TAG", "Completed in ${elapsed}ms")
}
options: Sequential: ~6 seconds total (2+3+1)
Parallel: ~3 seconds total (all three run concurrently, total = slowest)
Parallel: ~1 second total (fastest wins)
Error: cannot use multiple async in one scope
questions.explanation: All three async calls start immediately and run concurrently. .await() suspends until each result is ready, but since they're already running in parallel, the total time equals the slowest operation (~3 seconds). This is the "parallel decomposition" pattern — essential for loading multiple data sources efficiently.
hints: async starts the coroutine immediately, await just waits for the result
All three start at roughly the same time

questions.title: Exception Propagation in launch
questions.description: What happens when the inner coroutine throws an exception?
options: Only the failing child is cancelled, the second child completes normally
The exception propagates up: the failing child cancels the parent, which cancels ALL children including the second launch
The exception is silently swallowed
The app crashes with an unhandled exception dialog
questions.explanation: With launch, exceptions propagate upward through the scope hierarchy. The failing child notifies its parent, the parent cancels itself and ALL remaining children (structured concurrency). The second launch gets cancelled before it can print. This is by design — if part of your work fails, all related work should stop. Use supervisorScope if you want independent failure.
hints: Structured concurrency: parent and children are linked
Think about what the parent does when a child fails

questions.title: SupervisorScope for Independent Tasks
questions.description: This code loads a dashboard with three sections. If the "recommendations" API fails, the whole dashboard goes blank. Fix it so each section fails independently.

checks.message: Use supervisorScope or SupervisorJob to isolate failures

questions.explanation: supervisorScope changes the failure behavior: a child's failure does NOT cancel siblings. Combined with runCatching (or try/catch) around each call, each section can fail independently. The UI shows whatever data succeeded and handles nulls for failures. This is the standard pattern for dashboards with multiple independent data sources.
hints: Normal scope: one child fails → all fail. What scope type lets children fail independently?
Each async should handle its own errors

questions.title: withContext for Thread Switching
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to correctly switch from Main to IO thread for a network call, then back to Main to update UI.

blanks.hint: Switches the coroutine to a different dispatcher

questions.explanation: withContext(Dispatchers.IO) switches the coroutine to the IO dispatcher for the duration of the block, then automatically resumes on the original dispatcher (Main). It's a suspend function, so the code reads sequentially even though it switches threads. This is the standard pattern for calling blocking APIs from a coroutine on Main.
hints: This function suspends, switches context, runs the block, and switches back
Network calls should run on the IO dispatcher

questions.title: CoroutineExceptionHandler
options: All three options work
Only Option A works — CEH must be on the root/first coroutine in the scope hierarchy
Options A and B work, C does not
Only Option C works with async
questions.explanation: CoroutineExceptionHandler only works when installed on the root coroutine of the scope (Option A). In Option B, the exception propagates to the parent first — the handler on the child is ignored. In Option C, async holds the exception until .await(), which re-throws it as a regular exception — CEH does not catch it. For child coroutines, use try/catch instead.
hints: CEH is a "last resort" handler — it only works at the top level
Think about where exceptions propagate: up to the parent, not to the child's handler

questions.title: Cancellation Not Cooperative
questions.description: This coroutine cannot be cancelled — it keeps running even after the ViewModel is cleared. Fix it.

checks.message: Use delay() instead of Thread.sleep() — delay is cancellation-aware
Check for cancellation in the loop (isActive/ensureActive) or use delay()

questions.explanation: Thread.sleep() blocks the thread and is NOT cancellation-aware — the coroutine can't be cancelled during sleep. delay() is a suspend function that checks for cancellation. Also, while(true) should be while(isActive) to check cancellation between iterations. Coroutine cancellation is cooperative — your code must check for it.
hints: Thread.sleep() blocks the thread — the coroutine framework cannot interrupt it
There's a suspend-function equivalent of Thread.sleep()

questions.title: Structured Concurrency Rules
questions.description: Explain the three rules of structured concurrency in Kotlin coroutines and why they matter.
questions.answer: Structured concurrency has three rules: (1) When a parent scope is cancelled, all child coroutines are cancelled. (2) When a child coroutine fails with an exception, it cancels the parent (which cancels all siblings). (3) A parent scope does not complete until all child coroutines complete. These rules prevent coroutine leaks (orphaned coroutines running after their parent is gone), ensure cleanup on failure, and make concurrent code predictable — you always know the lifetime of your coroutines.
questions.explanation: These three rules prevent "coroutine leaks" — the concurrent equivalent of memory leaks. Without structured concurrency, you could launch a coroutine and forget about it, leaving it running after the Activity/ViewModel is destroyed. The scope hierarchy (viewModelScope → child → grandchild) ensures everything has a defined lifetime.
hints: Think about what happens when: (a) parent is cancelled, (b) child fails, (c) parent finishes
Why does this prevent "leaks"?

questions.title: coroutineScope vs supervisorScope
questions.description: What's the difference in behavior between these two blocks?
options: Both behave the same
Block A: orders fails → users is cancelled → whole function throws. Block B: orders catches its error → users completes → function returns with empty orders list
Block A works, Block B crashes
Block B is slower because it doesn't cancel early
questions.explanation: coroutineScope follows standard structured concurrency — one child fails, everything fails. supervisorScope isolates children — one child's failure doesn't affect siblings. In Block B, fetchOrders() failing is caught locally, so users completes normally. Use coroutineScope for "all or nothing" work, supervisorScope for independent tasks.
hints: The key difference is what happens to OTHER children when one fails

questions.title: Retry with Exponential Backoff
questions.description: Write a generic retryWithBackoff suspend function that retries a block up to maxRetries times with exponential delay (1s, 2s, 4s...). Return the result on success, throw on final failure.

checks.message: Use a loop for retry attempts

questions.explanation: This is a common production pattern. repeat(maxRetries - 1) tries N-1 times with catching. The final call runs without catch, letting the exception propagate. delay() is cancellation-aware, so the retry respects scope cancellation. Doubling the delay implements exponential backoff, reducing pressure on failing services.
hints: Try the block, catch failures, delay, increase the delay, repeat
The last attempt should not catch — let the caller handle the final failure

questions.title: Mutex Reentrancy Trap
questions.description: A coroutine holds a Mutex and calls a suspend function that tries to acquire the same Mutex. What happens?
options: It works fine — Mutex allows the same coroutine to re-acquire
It throws an IllegalStateException immediately
DEADLOCK — the coroutine suspends forever waiting for itself to release the lock
The inner lock is skipped automatically
questions.explanation: Kotlin's Mutex is NOT reentrant, unlike Java's ReentrantLock. When inner() tries to acquire the Mutex that outer() already holds, the coroutine suspends waiting for the lock to be released — but it will never be released because the same coroutine is the one holding it. This is a silent deadlock: no exception, no crash, just a coroutine that hangs forever. If you need reentrant locking, use ReentrantLock (but be aware it blocks the thread) or restructure your code to avoid nested lock acquisition.
hints: Mutex is NOT like Java's ReentrantLock — it does not track which coroutine holds it
What happens when you try to acquire a lock that is already held?

questions.title: 10,000 Concurrent Network Calls
questions.description: You have 10,000 concurrent network calls. Why would coroutines + Dispatchers.IO handle this better than a ThreadPoolExecutor with 10,000 threads?
questions.answer: Memory: 10,000 threads x ~1 MB stack each = ~10 GB just for stacks. 10,000 coroutines x ~few hundred bytes each = ~few MB. Threads BLOCK while waiting for network IO, holding their entire stack in memory and occupying an OS thread. Coroutines SUSPEND, saving a tiny state object to the heap and releasing the thread to run other coroutines. Context switching 10,000 threads causes massive kernel overhead (~1-10 microseconds per switch, TLB/cache flushes). Coroutine switching is userspace-only (~100 nanoseconds, just swapping a state machine object). Dispatchers.IO uses a limited thread pool (default 64 threads) that efficiently multiplexes 10,000 suspended coroutines — only the ones actively doing work occupy a thread.
questions.explanation: This is the fundamental advantage of coroutines: they decouple concurrency from threads. With threads, each concurrent operation requires a dedicated OS thread (expensive). With coroutines, each concurrent operation is just a small heap object that borrows a thread only when it has CPU work to do. During network IO (which is mostly waiting), the coroutine suspends and the thread serves other coroutines. The 64-thread Dispatchers.IO pool can efficiently handle 10,000+ concurrent IO operations because at any given moment, most coroutines are suspended (waiting for network responses), and only a few are actively running.
hints: How much memory does each thread's stack consume vs. a coroutine's state?
What does each thread do while waiting for a network response? What about a coroutine?
